en.extract:
The Chiloé wigeon (Mareca sibilatrix), also known as the southern wigeon, is one of three extant species of wigeon in the genus Mareca of the dabbling duck subfamily. This bird is indigenous to the southern part of South America, including the Chiloé Archipelago, from which it gets its name.
In its native range, it is called the pato overo ("piebald duck") or pato real ("royal duck"), although the latter name also refers to the Muscovy in the wild. Its specific epithet, sibilatrix, means 'whistler', referring to the bird's call. 


Description
The Chiloé wigeon has a body length of 46 to 56 cm (18 to 22 in) and a wingspan of 75 to 86 cm (30 to 34 in). The wing length is about 25 cm (9.8 in) and the weight is approximately 800 g (28 oz).
This bird has an iridescent green-blue cap on its head, and a bluish gray bill with a black tip. The cheeks and forehead are white, the eyes are dark brown, and there is a white auricular patch. The neck and occipital part of the head are black. The breast is barred black and white and the plumage of the wings is gray and white. The flanks of males are rust colored, and light brown on females. The legs and feet are gray.
Sexual dimorphism is relatively subtle in this species of wigeon. Males are usually somewhat larger and heavier, and with somewhat brighter plumage and more strongly pronounced iridescence of the cap. Apart from these features, it can be difficult to distinguish the two sexes. Juveniles resemble adult birds, but the rust coloring on the flanks is diminished or absent.


Distribution and habitat
This duck is indigenous to the southern part of South America, where it is found on freshwater lakes, marshes, shallow lagoons and slow flowing rivers. Vagrants have been observed in South Georgia, South Orkney and the South Shetland Islands.
It breeds primarily in Argentina, Uruguay, and Chile. The northern border of the breeding range is in Argentina at 36° S and Chile at 40° S. It also breeds sparingly in the Falkland Islands. It migrates to southeastern Brazil for the winter.
It was first introduced to Europe in 1870; it soon bred in zoos.
Because the Chiloé wigeon is widely distributed and has a large, stable wild population, it is categorized as least concern by the IUCN.


Ecology and behavior
The Chiloé wigeon displays a variety of behaviors depending on habitat. It is an omnivore, feeding predominantly on aquatic plants and grass, and occasionally coastal algae.
This monogamous species breeds in the austral spring, between September and December. Pairs inhabit very small breeding territories, building their nests in grasses and under bushes. The female lays eight to ten white or cream-colored eggs. After a gestation period of approximately 24–25 days the ducklings are born. The father helps raise the ducklings; however, he leaves the family after the offspring molt.
Captive hybrids with the Philippine Duck (Anas luzonica) have been described.


Parasites
This species is host to a variety of parasitic species which include chewing lice, feather mites, nasal mites, tapeworms, nematodes and trematodes.


References


Works cited
Kolbe, Hartmut; The Ducks of the World, Ulmer Verlag 1999, ISBN 3-8001-7442-1
Woods, A, Woods, R: Atlas of Breeding Birds of the Falkland Islands, Anthony Nelson, Shropshire 1997, ISBN 0-904614-60-3


External links

Anas sibilatrix videos, photos & sounds on the Internet Bird Collection
Sea World

The '''Chiloé wigeon''' ('''''Mareca sibilatrix'''''), also known as the '''southern wigeon''', is one of three [[Extant taxon|extant]] species of [[wigeon]] in the [[genus]] ''Mareca'' of the [[Anatinae|dabbling duck]] subfamily. This bird is [[Indigenous (ecology)|indigenous]] to the southern part of [[South America]], including the [[Chiloé Archipelago]], from which it gets its name.
In its native range, it is called the {{lang|es|pato [[wikt:overo|overo]]}} ("[[piebald#Other animals|piebald]] duck") or {{lang|es|pato real}} ("royal duck"), although the latter name also refers to the [[Muscovy duck|Muscovy]] in the wild. Its [[specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]], ''[[wikt:sibilatrix|sibilatrix]]'', means 'whistler', referring to the bird's call. 

==Description==


The Chiloé wigeon has a body length of {{convert|46|to|56|cm|in|abbr=on}} and a [[wingspan]] of {{convert|75|to|86|cm|in|abbr=on}}. The wing length is about {{convert|25|cm|in|abbr=on}} and the weight is approximately {{convert|800|g|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Heinzel1977>{{cite book|last1=Heinzel|first1=H|last2=Fitter|first2=RSR|last3=Parslow|first3=JLF|title=The birds of Britain and Europe with North Africa and the Middle East|edition=4th|page=52|publisher=Collins|location=London|year=1979|isbn=978-0-00-219234-7}}</ref><ref name=Ogilvie1999>{{cite book|last1=Ogilvie|first1=MA|last2=Young|first2=S|title=Photographic handbook of the wildfowl of the world|publisher=New Holland Books|location=Frenchs Forest, New South Wales|year=1999|isbn=978-1-85368-625-2}}</ref>

This bird has an [[Iridescence|iridescent]] green-blue cap on its head, and a bluish gray [[Beak|bill]] with a black tip. The cheeks and forehead are white, the eyes are dark brown, and there is a white auricular patch. The neck and [[Occiput|occipital]] part of the head are black. The breast is barred black and white and the [[plumage]] of the wings is gray and white. The flanks of males are [[Rust (color)|rust]] colored, and light brown on females. The legs and feet are gray.

[[Sexual dimorphism]] is relatively subtle in this species of wigeon. Males are usually somewhat larger and heavier, and with somewhat brighter plumage and more strongly pronounced iridescence of the cap. Apart from these features, it can be difficult to distinguish the two sexes.<ref name=Shirihai2002>Shirihai H: ''A Complete Guide to Antarctic Wildlife - The Birds and Marine Mammals of the Antarctic Continent and Southern Ocean'', Alula Press, Degerby 2002, p. 247. {{ISBN|951-98947-0-5}}</ref> Juveniles resemble adult birds, but the rust coloring on the flanks is diminished or absent.

<gallery mode = packed heights = 160px>
Mareca sibilatrix MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.32.5.jpg| ''Mareca sibilatrix'' - [[MHNT]]
Zürich - Seefeld - Zürichhorn IMG 8475.JPG|Searching for food
Anas sibilatrix -Puerto Natales, Patagonia, Chile -family-8 (1).jpg|Family in [[Patagonia]]
</gallery>

==Distribution and habitat==

This duck is indigenous to the southern part of South America, where it is found on freshwater lakes, marshes, shallow lagoons and slow flowing rivers. [[Vagrancy (biology)|Vagrants]] have been observed in [[South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands|South Georgia]], [[South Orkney Islands|South Orkney]] and the [[South Shetland Islands]].

It breeds primarily in Argentina, Uruguay, and Chile. The northern border of the breeding range is in Argentina at 36° S and Chile at 40° S. It also breeds sparingly in the [[Falkland Islands]].<ref>Wood, p. 86</ref> It migrates to southeastern Brazil for the winter.<ref name=Clements2007>Clements J (2007) [https://www.cornellpress.cornell.edu/cup_detail.taf?ti_id=4566 ''The Clements Checklist of the Birds of the World''], Cornell University Press, Ithaca</ref>

It was first introduced to Europe in 1870; it soon bred in zoos.

Because the Chiloé wigeon is widely distributed and has a large, stable wild population, it is categorized as [[least concern]] by the IUCN.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

==Ecology and behavior==

The Chiloé wigeon displays a variety of behaviors depending on habitat. It is an [[omnivore]], feeding predominantly on aquatic plants and grass, and occasionally coastal [[algae]].<ref name=Shirihai2002/>

This monogamous species breeds in the austral spring, between September and December. Pairs inhabit very small breeding territories, building their nests in grasses and under bushes. The female lays eight to ten white or cream-colored eggs. After a gestation period of approximately 24–25 days the ducklings are born. The father helps raise the ducklings; however, he leaves the family after the offspring [[Moulting|molt]].

Captive hybrids with the [[Philippine duck|Philippine Duck]] (''Anas luzonica'') have been described.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Ottenburghs|first1=Jente|last2=Harteman|first2=Jan|title=Sexually dichromatic hybrids between two monochromatic duck species, the Chiloé wigeon and the Philippine duck|journal=Ecology and Evolution|year=2021|volume=11|issue=23|pages=16409–16412|language=en|doi=10.1002/ece3.8253|pmid=34938444|pmc=8668737|bibcode=2021EcoEv..1116409O |issn=2045-7758}}</ref>
<gallery mode = packed heights = 160px>
Chiloe wigeon (Mareca sibilatrix) in flight Maipo.jpg
Chiloe wigeons (Mareca sibilatrix) in flight Maipo.jpg
</gallery>

=== Parasites ===
This species is host to a variety of parasitic species which include [[chewing lice]], [[feather mite]]s, [[nasal mite]]s, [[tapeworm]]s, [[nematode]]s and [[Trematoda|trematodes]].<ref>{{Cite journal|title=The parasitic fauna of the Chiloe wigeon Mareca sibilatrix (Anseriformes: Anatidae) from Ñuble region, Chile|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2405939025000048|journal=Veterinary Parasitology: Regional Studies and Reports|date=2025-01-01|issn=2405-9390|article-number=101197|volume=57|doi=10.1016/j.vprsr.2025.101197|first=Camilo H.|last=Salazar-Silva|first2=Lucas|last2=Aravena|first3=Martín|last3=Zamorano-Uribe|first4=Javier|last4=Andrade-Hernández|first5=María Carolina|last5=Silva-de la Fuente|first6=Armando|last6=Cicchino|first7=Sergey|last7=Mironov|first8=Lucila|last8=Moreno|first9=Pablo|last9=Oyarzún-Ruiz|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

zh.extract:
黑白斑胸鸭（学名：Mareca sibilatrix），是雁形目鸭科的一种鸟类。


特征
黑白斑胸鸭体重约923.16克，翼长约245毫米，嘴峰长约37.6毫米，喙宽度约15.2毫米，喙厚度约12.2毫米，跗蹠长约36.3毫米，尾长约83.8毫米。黑白斑胸鸭是部分迁徙的鸟类，其栖息在开放的湖泊、沼泽等湿地环境中，食性为草食性。


分布
南美洲南部和福克兰群岛；冬季到巴西东南部。
